turns.content: Just got home after overtime, and my head is still buzzing. Actually, I feel most alert in the morning. Every day at 7:30 AM, I have breakfast while playing BBC Radio 3 in the background; it's become my fixed ritual to kickstart the workday.

That sense of ritual is wonderful! Having breakfast accompanied by classical music really helps one settle down. Do you happen to handle some work during this time, or is it purely for enjoying the music?

I definitely review blueprints. Every morning at 7:30, I'm used to having a simple breakfast with BBC Radio 3 playing in the background, while flipping through the day's architectural drawings. This dual input of visual and auditory stimuli helps me establish a sense of rational order before even stepping into the office.

So for you, music is not just about relaxation, but also the cornerstone for building work logic. That 'sense of order' sounds very much in line with an architect's way of thinking. However, facing rigorous lines and structures all day, do you ever feel the urge to break that balance occasionally?

Haha, you caught me. Actually, there's a 'king of destruction' in my house: my cousin Lin Zihao. He always thinks classical music is outdated and prefers his electric guitar and rock bands. But when we try pairing my improvised piano accompaniment with his distorted riffs, the collision between classical harmony and modern noise unexpectedly creates a sense of architectural space.

Wow, that creates such a vivid image! The elegance of the piano and the agitation of the electric guitar sound like completely conflicting elements, yet they manage to fuse into a 'sense of space'? How exactly do you coordinate that?

It's really just messing around. My cousin Lin Zihao is an electric guitar fanatic who always says classical music is too old-school. But last time, when he did an improvised solo with distorted riffs and I followed up with improvised piano accompaniment, the collision between noise and harmony actually sparked a burst of inspiration, and I recorded a demo right on the spot.

That's so cool! This kind of cross-style improvisation often sparks the most inspiration. Did you end up using that demo in any of your design projects? It feels like music with that kind of conflicting beauty would be perfect for some avant-garde architectural spaces.

Not officially yet, but I've been pondering how to transform it into ambient music. As you know, I like to compose my own background scores when working on proposals. That order within chaos is actually quite similar to our approach when dealing with complex site relationships.

Indeed, architecture and design are arts of synesthesia. From the rational order of BBC Radio 3 in the morning to the emotional release of playing rock with your cousin at night, your day is full of tension.

Exactly, life needs that kind of contrast. If I only listened to Chopin or Debussy every day, I might have become too 'ethereal' and detached from reality. Occasionally, I need the direct noise from someone like Lin Zihao to pull me back to earth.

Haha, 'too ethereal' is a very vivid description. It seems your cousin is not just your playmate but also your 'grounding wire'. Next time you jam together, could I be a virtual spectator? I'm curious about that sound effect.

No problem, I'll record it for you next time he comes over for rehearsal. Actually, although he acts tough, he really admires my ability to improvise and keep up with his key. After all, even rock musicians need some music theory support; otherwise, it's just noise, which isn't pleasant.

That's the importance of professional foundation! The basics you built from practicing piano since childhood really show in moments like these. Being able to master the delicacy of classical music while embracing the wildness of rock—that's true musical literacy.

You're too kind. Actually, I'm also learning from his attitude of 'not caring about perfection, only caring about emotion'. Sometimes when I get too bogged down in details while drawing, I think of how intoxicated and crazy he looks when playing guitar, and tell myself not to be so tense.

That kind of mindset adjustment is precious. Architects often pursue extreme precision, but artistic creation sometimes indeed needs a spark of 'loss of control'. It seems you two cousins are achieving greatness together.

You could even say we're torturing each other, haha. He thinks I'm long-winded, and I think he's noisy. But the moment the piano and electric guitar sounds blend together, it feels like everything is worth it. That resonance is quite magical.

That's the charm of music; it can transcend differences in age and style to connect different people. Have you ever thought about using this 'piano plus electric guitar' combination for the opening performance of your future architectural exhibitions?

Hey, that's a great idea! For my graduation exhibition, I played the piano alone. But if I come up with a new proposal this time, maybe I really can rope Lin Zihao in for a live 'Architectural Rock' performance. Just imagining that scene feels quite subversive.

It will absolutely blow the crowd away! From the classical order of dawn to the rock impulse of night, and then fusing them into your architectural design—that in itself is a fantastic story. Looking forward to your 'Architectural Rock' debut!
Finally home! I was so crushed on Hangzhou Metro Line 1 today that I started questioning my existence; I felt like I was being flattened.
Haha, Line 1 during rush hour is indeed a 'battlefield.' But I remember you mentioning before that you have a 'secret weapon' to deal with all that noise during your commute?

Exactly, my noise-canceling headphones are my barrier against the chaos. I switch playlists with the same precision as tuning architectural lighting: when I'm overwhelmed revising plans, I listen to Philip Glass's minimalism to 'noise-cancel' my stress; when I'm relaxed, I switch to City Pop to lighten my step.

What a brilliant analogy! Using the repetitive melodies of minimalism to soothe the anxiety of revising plans really fits your architect's logical mindset. So, which mood were you switching into today?

Today actually went quite smoothly; the client didn't give me much trouble. So while squeezing onto Metro Line 1, if I had been frustrated with revisions, I would have listened to Philip Glass's minimalism to calm down; but since I was in a good mood, I switched to City Pop and swayed to the rhythm. Today was definitely the latter.

That creates such a vivid image! Being physically stuck in a crowded carriage while your ears are filled with upbeat urban rhythms. Doesn't that contrast make the commute home feel much less unbearable?

Absolutely! Especially on a Friday evening, squeezed in the crowds on Metro Line 1, just putting on my headphones and switching to a City Pop playlist transforms everything. Those upbeat basslines and urban-themed lyrics instantly turn the cramped carriage into a flowing neon street, making me feel incredibly relaxed and content.

'A flowing neon street'—what a poetic description! It seems City Pop is truly your healing balm. But your City Pop playlist must be full of hidden gems, especially since you have a friend with impeccable taste in records nearby.

Hehe, you guessed it. When I'm stuck on Metro Line 1, I often listen to City Pop to ease the irritation of the crowds. But speaking of City Pop, I have to thank Susu; she always digs out out-of-print singles from vinyl records that I've never heard before and recommends them to me.

Susu truly lives up to her reputation as that 'illustrator who doesn't play an instrument but has exquisite taste in record collecting.' Did you two fall into your usual routine again, drinking wine and listening to these vinyls at her place over the weekend while you explained the background stories?

Yes, that's exactly how last Saturday went. She pulled out an early Mariya Takeuchi vinyl; the sound quality had that unique warm graininess that's just different from digital music. At that moment, I was thinking how great it would be to blend that texture into the background music for the lounge area in my next design.

Integrating the analog warmth of vinyl into the acoustic design of architectural spaces—that's so 'Lin Xiaoyun'! Just like you did at your graduation exhibition, combining music with architectural circulation. Susu must be thrilled to hear about this idea.

She is very supportive. But speaking of spatial acoustics, Ah Jie asked me yesterday if I could record a piano sample for his new white noise project; he wants something with an urban night atmosphere. I'm wondering if I should incorporate some City Pop chord colors?

That crossover collaboration sounds fascinating! Using the piano to interpret City Pop chord colors for white noise creates both an urban groove and a sense of tranquility. Are you planning to record it on your Yamaha U1 in the study?

Yes, right in the study. The high-fidelity speakers in the corner help me monitor the details. Actually, this kind of creation reminds me of when I was a child and my grandfather would play the erhu while telling me stories; music has always been a companion, whether by West Lake or on the subway.

From your grandfather's erhu to today's City Pop piano samples, the form of music changes, but the core essence of 'companionship' remains constant. That's precisely why you want music to accompany you throughout your life, even if you don't become a professional musician.

Exactly. By the way, while I was practicing piano this Wednesday night, my cousin Zihao ran over to argue with me about whether 'classical music is outdated.' He insisted on jamming his electric guitar with my Debussy 'Clair de Lune,' and surprisingly, it harmonized beautifully; the hazy impressionism clashing with the distortion effect created a whole new flavor.

Haha, a collision between a post-05s rock teen and a classical pianist! Debussy's impressionist colors are already quite hazy, and adding electric guitar distortion might just create a modern 'urban impressionism.' Did you record that improvisation?

Yes, I recorded it; it's saved on my phone. Maybe someday while watching a show at MAO Livehouse, inspiration will strike, and I can develop it into a full piece. My current state is shuttling between construction sites and meeting rooms during the day, and finding myself again in music at night.

That is the best state of life: architecture is your skeleton, and music is your soul. I hope your City Pop moments on Metro Line 1 next week continue to recharge you with energy. I look forward to hearing new works from you and Zihao or Ah Jie!

Just finished a day of drafting and returned to my study. Seeing the Yamaha U1 in the corner, I suddenly feel reluctant to move. This is the third time this week I've wanted to slack off, but remembering that Professor Zhang will be coming next week to hear my lesson, I know I have to bite the bullet and practice.

Haha, it seems Professor Zhang's authority is still very much intact! After all, a "review" every two months is indeed nerve-wracking. Which piece are you mainly working on lately? Is it still that Debussy piece?

Yes, it's still "Clair de Lune." Actually, I have a bit of an obsessive-compulsive habit: my practice times on Wednesday and Friday evenings are set in stone. I must start with half an hour of Hanon exercises to warm up. Only when my fingers are fully limber do I dare to touch Debussy's delicate tones; otherwise, I feel like I'm letting down Professor Zhang's teachings.

That sense of ritual is very architect-like, emphasizing structure and process. But once you finish warming up and dive into the piece, the feeling must be quite different, right? Did Professor Zhang have any new feedback for you this time?

Don't even get me started; I got scolded again during the last lesson. He always says that when I play Debussy's "Clair de Lune," I have technique in abundance but lack emotion. He says I'm too much like an architect pursuing structural precision, forgetting that Impressionist music requires that elusive haziness and use of negative space. Listening to him, I'm starting to doubt whether I'm treating the piano like a ruler and compass.

Although Professor Zhang's words were sharp, they really hit the essence of Impressionism. Structural precision is your strength, but perhaps sometimes you need to deliberately "blur" the boundaries? Besides practicing at home, have you tried changing your environment to find some inspiration?

Actually, yes. Since I'm always told at home that my playing feels like solving math problems, lacking that hazy atmosphere, I've started going to West Lake on weekends. I bring a Bluetooth speaker, listen to Debussy while watching the lake water, trying to recapture that synesthesia where vision and hearing merge. Maybe this will satisfy Professor Zhang a bit more at the next lesson.

That's a fantastic idea! Listening to "Reflets dans l'eau" or "Clair de Lune" by West Lake, watching the real shimmering waves, that synesthetic experience must be far more profound than just playing dryly in the study. The city of Hangzhou gives your music a unique boost.

Right! Especially in the evening when the light fades and the outline of the lake becomes blurred; in that moment, I suddenly felt I understood what Debussy was aiming for. But back to reality, I still have my immutable practice sessions on Wednesday and Friday evenings: half an hour of Hanon warm-ups, then grinding away at "Clair de Lune." Teacher Zhang always says my technique is sufficient, but that hazy tone quality needs more polishing. I hope I can bring what I felt by the lake back to the keys.

Bringing outdoor perceptions into indoor practice is itself a high-level process of re-creation. I believe that next time Professor Zhang hears the "lake light and moon color" in your playing, he will be impressed. Besides "Clair de Lune," have you tried any other Impressionist works recently to help find that feeling?

For now, I'm still grinding away at this one piece. After all, Professor Zhang says that mastering one piece thoroughly is more useful than playing ten superficially. Moreover, every time I sit in front of my Yamaha U1 now, I close my eyes first to recall the image of the West Lake waves before placing my fingers on the keys. It feels much more vivid than the mechanical state I was in after just doing Hanon warm-ups before.

This is what they call "having a well-formed plan in mind," except in your case, your mind is filled with the moonlight over West Lake. This ability to transform life experiences into artistic expression is actually similar to what you do in architectural design; both are about capturing space and atmosphere.

You're absolutely right! Actually, I feel that music and architecture are essentially both about dealing with the relationship between time and space. It's just that Professor Zhang always thinks I'm too rational. Probably because I come from an architecture background, I subconsciously analyze the structure of the score, ignoring the dreamlike atmosphere that Debussy wanted.

Rationality is the foundation; sensibility is the sublimation. You already have solid technique (after all, you practice Hanon so rigorously); now you just need to release a bit of your inner sensibility. By the way, during your practice this Friday, why not try dimming the lights in your study a bit to simulate the evening light at West Lake?

Wow, that's a great idea! I'll try it tonight. Actually, I was also wondering if my home study is just too familiar an environment, lacking that sense of novelty. The synesthesia I felt listening to "Reflets dans l'eau" by West Lake was precisely triggered by the change in environment.

Environmental psychology applies to music practice as well. Changing even a small environmental variable can bring about unexpected chemical reactions. I look forward to your feedback after Friday's practice; maybe next time Professor Zhang will say, "Xiaoyun, your 'Moonlight' has finally 'brightened' up."

Thanks for the good wishes! If I can really satisfy him, I'll have to thank the waters of West Lake properly. Actually, thinking about it, Professor Zhang criticizing me for playing "Clair de Lune" like doing math problems is also his way of hoping I can step out of my comfort zone and experience a more diverse world.

Exactly, a mentor's strictness often hides the deepest expectations. Moreover, you already have great perceptiveness; whether it's in architectural design or observing natural scenery, these are your unique musical nutrients. As long as you blend them into your piano sound, that will be the unique "Lin Xiaoyun version" of Debussy.

Haha, the "Lin Xiaoyun version" of Debussy, I like that title! Tonight, I'll go to the study first, finish my Hanon exercises according to the old rules, and then try to replay the scenes of West Lake in my mind while playing "Clair de Lune." I hope to find that balance point between hazy and clear.

Go for it! Imagine that what flows from your fingertips isn't just notes, but the swaying moonlight over West Lake. Wish you a pleasant practice session tonight; you might even have a sudden burst of inspiration. Remember to record it!

Sigh, the proposal for that commercial complex was rejected by the client again today. Even after five revisions, they still say it 'lacks emotional tension.' I'm completely collapsed on the sofa right now; I just want to turn off the lights and be alone for a while.

Sending you a hug, Xiaoyun. Revising something five times in a row is truly draining. That feeling of being rejected without a clear reason is the most disheartening. How about giving yourself a break and doing something to help you relax?

Exactly, I'm in no state to think rationally right now. When I feel particularly devastated by a rejected proposal, I don't force myself to cheer up. Instead, I put on my headphones and listen alone to Chopin's 'Nocturnes' or Rachmaninoff's adagios, allowing myself to immerse in that grand melancholy, letting my tears and the music vent together.

This kind of 'fighting fire with fire' approach to venting can actually be very healing. Chopin's Nocturnes indeed possess a gentleness that can wrap around all your grievances. Are you currently practicing a specific Nocturne, or just picking one at random to listen to?

I'm currently grinding away at Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major. Actually, every couple of months I have to go to Professor Zhang's house to 'get scolded' during my lesson. Last time I played this Chopin 'Nocturne,' I was criticized again for lacking delicate emotion. He said my technique was too full, as if I were 'constructing' the music rather than letting it 'flow.' It seems I still have more pondering to do.

Professor Zhang's standards are indeed as high as ever; after all, he wants you to break through your bottleneck. However, the comment about 'playing piano like constructing' must be a bit too piercing for you as an architectural designer, haha. What do you think the feeling of 'flowing' should be like?

It really was piercing! But thinking about it carefully, it might be because I focus too much on structure. Although Chopin is a representative of Western Romanticism, every time I play the 'Nocturnes,' I'm reminded of the chanting quality when my grandfather played the erhu. That Eastern beauty of linear melody actually shares a similar effect with Chopin's ornamentation.

That's such a unique perspective! Connecting Chopin's ornamentation with the chanting quality of the erhu. Did your grandfather often play the erhu in front of you when you were young? Has this memory subtly influenced your understanding of melody?

Yes, the sight of my grandfather, Lin Jianguo, playing the erhu when I was a child is my most beautiful memory of melody. His favorite piece to play was 'The Moon Reflected in the Second Spring.' Back then, I didn't understand it; I just felt the sound was like someone sighing yet also singing. Strangely enough, now when I play Chopin's 'Nocturnes,' I always feel that those ornamentations hide the unique Eastern linear melodic beauty of the erhu. Maybe this is the enlightenment my grandfather gave me.

That's so moving—a cross-cultural resonance. Perhaps what Professor Zhang meant by 'lack of emotion' was exactly the missing flavor of this 'Eastern linearity' stemming from your life experience? Next time you have your lesson, try bringing that sense of breath your grandfather had when playing the erhu into your fingertips and see what happens?

You're right! I was previously too entangled in fingering and dynamics markings, forgetting the most essential singability of music. When my grandfather gave me that old erhu back in the day, he said, 'Music is companionship, not competition.' I think I really need to find that feeling of 'speaking' on the keys again.

'Music is companionship, not competition'—that is truly a golden quote. It seems today's disappointment has instead helped you find a direction for breakthrough. Are you going to continue listening to Nocturnes tonight, or change the mood and listen to some rock music that your cousin Lin Zihao likes?

Haha, let's not go there. If I listen to rock music now, I'm afraid I won't be able to resist debating with Lin Zihao about whether 'classical music is outdated,' which would be too mentally exhausting. Tonight, I'd rather quietly listen to Op. 9 No. 2 one more time, trying to simulate the bowing trajectory of the erhu in my mind as I touch the keys.

That practice method is very visual! Using the sense of lines in architecture to understand the bowing of the erhu, and then using the piano to recreate it, feels like you will create a very unique timbre. If you get the chance, you should really record it and send it to Professor Zhang; he might be pleasantly surprised.

Thanks for the good wishes! If I can get scolded a bit less next time I visit him, I'll be thanking my lucky stars. By the way, speaking of lines, I'm recently designing a cultural center project, and it suddenly occurred to me: could I integrate this East-West fused melodic concept into the spatial acoustic design, making the building itself feel like a Nocturne?

Wow, that idea is fantastic! Just like your master's graduation exhibition, perfectly blending music and architecture. Imagine playing Chopin with an erhu flavor within that space; what a wonderful experience that would be. Do you need me to help you find some relevant acoustic case studies?

No need, I'll ponder it myself first, and also ask Ah Jie. He's a sound designer and knows much more about spatial acoustics than I do. However, I've already found the core inspiration: it's that sense of 'linear' flow, no longer being a space cut into blocks.

Collaborating with Ah Jie will surely spark something great. It seems having this proposal rejected wasn't such a bad thing after all; instead, it inspired such a brilliant creative idea from you. Is your mood feeling even a little bit better?

Much better! Indeed, music and chatting are the best therapies. I don't plan to work overtime on revising drawings tonight. Instead, I'm going to sit by West Lake, bring a speaker to play Debussy's 'Reflets dans l'eau,' watch the real lake water, and think about my 'Eastern Nocturne' architecture.

You really know how to enjoy life! Listening to Debussy by West Lake is simply the ultimate synesthetic experience. I wish you an explosion of inspiration tonight. Perhaps next time you meet Professor Zhang, you can impress him with this 'Nocturne' full of Eastern flavor!

The weather this weekend is absolutely perfect. Susu and I ducked into that hidden independent record store tucked away in an alley on Zhongshan North Road. You know how much I love doing this on weekend afternoons; it feels like giving my brain a deep clean.
That sounds so cozy! The unique scent of old wood mixed with vinyl in those vintage record stores really helps calm the mind. Did Susu dig up any treasure tracks this time?

Absolutely! Even though she doesn't understand music theory or play an instrument, her taste in selecting tracks is simply phenomenal. Today, she picked up an obscure 80s Japanese City Pop album. The melodic lines are incredibly laid-back, perfectly compensating for the limitations in my auditory perspective as someone with an academic background.

Haha, Susu is basically your exclusive 'auditory curator.' Does that characteristic sense of urban alienation in City Pop remind you of any specific architectural spaces? Perhaps glass-walled buildings with a retro filter?

You guessed it! These days, hunting for vinyl isn't just about listening to music for me. I've made it a habit to carry a small notebook. Whenever I hear something special, like minimalism or City Pop, I immediately jot down potential architectural scenarios where it might fit—for instance, noting that a certain melody would work well in a museum's lounge area, or that a specific rhythm suits the lobby of an office building.

Translating auditory experiences into spatial language really fits your identity as an architectural designer. With such a 'synesthetic' library of materials accumulated over time, won't future design proposals come to you effortlessly?

Exactly, and sometimes inspiration strikes with incredible intensity. I remember hearing a strange piece of ambient music in the store last time; suddenly, a brilliant spiral staircase circulation flow appeared in my mind. I was so excited I couldn't sit still. I rushed home straight to the piano and hammered out a bunch of dissonant chords, trying to capture the rhythm of that epiphany.
Those instantaneous sparks are the most precious! Did you record it back then, or did it remain only in your memory? After all, improvised moments vanish in a flash.

Of course I recorded it! Now it's almost a reflex; whenever I get that surge of excitement at home, I immediately record myself on my phone after playing. That recording later became the core motif for the ambient music demo I created for a cultural center proposal. Although it was only a few dozen seconds long, the emotion was exactly right.

That's fantastic! From a chance listen in a vinyl shop, to an improvised capture at the piano, and finally integrating it into architectural design—the entire loop is simply perfect. It seems Susu dragging you to visit record stores isn't just for relaxation; she's actually 'recharging' your design batteries.

Exactly. She always says I don't know how to live, only knowing how to draft and play piano. But she doesn't realize that while I'm listening to those records, I'm already 'constructing' in my mind. By the way, I also found an early version of a Debussy record today. The timbre is particularly hazy, feeling like a perfect match for the 'Clair de Lune' I've been practicing recently.

Debussy's 'Clair de Lune' strives for that dappled, hazy sensation of light and shadow. Doesn't that share a similar essence with the changes in light and shadow you consider when designing architectural schemes?

Absolutely! Sometimes I feel architecture and music are truly interconnected. Just like Professor Zhang often criticizes me for having 'excessive technique but insufficient emotion,' perhaps in my designs I focus too much on structure and function, neglecting that moving sense of 'atmosphere.'

Professor Zhang is just eager for you to succeed. But by using music to nourish your design now and seeking that sense of atmosphere, aren't you precisely making up for that? Are there any other treasures in today's haul suitable for 'background music'?

There's an ambient music compilation. A few tracks inside have an incredibly strong sense of space, sounding like they're echoing in a vast concrete void. I plan to play them on a Bluetooth speaker in the conference room next week before meeting the client at the office, just to set the rhythm for the presentation.

That's a brilliant move! Using music to make a strong first impression allows the client to adjust their mood before even entering your design context. Looks like I'll have to try playing some Ryuichi Sakamoto before my next meeting too.

Haha, Ryuichi Sakamoto's 'Async' is definitely a magical tool. But speaking of this, I still miss the days when my grandfather played the erhu for me and told stories about 'Erquan Yingyue' (Moon Reflected in the Second Spring). That pure expression of emotion is something no vinyl record can ever replace.

Musical memories with family are indeed the most unique foundation. Your grandfather's erhu sounds might be the most primal source of your current perception of 'melodic beauty.' This also explains why your designs always carry a warmth of humanistic care.

What you said warms my heart. Alright, no more chatting for now. I need to hurry back and organize the inspiration I noted in my little notebook today. I'll also see if I can adapt that City Pop melody on the piano into a piece of background music suitable for the atrium of a commercial complex.

Go ahead, I'm looking forward to hearing your adaptation! I have a feeling your architectural proposals paired with your own composed music will stun the audience just like they did at your master's graduation exhibition. Have a wonderful weekend, Xiaoyun!

The weather in Hangzhou this weekend was absolutely perfect; I just came back from the West Lake. While sitting on a bench lost in thought, it suddenly struck me that Debussy's "Reflets dans l'eau" was written exactly for a moment like this.
Wow, just imagining that scene feels so healing! Did you bring a speaker specifically to play it? Listening to this piece by a real lake must feel completely different from listening at home.

Exactly, I brought a portable Bluetooth speaker with me. It wasn't really for anyone else to hear; I just sat there, gazing at the shimmering reflections on the lake surface while Debussy's melody filled my ears. That overlapping sensation of sight and sound was truly a perfect synesthetic experience.

"The overlapping of sight and sound"—that description is so vivid! As an architect, does this kind of synesthesia directly spark your design inspiration? Perhaps in how you handle light and shadow or facades?

Absolutely! The blurred boundaries in Impressionist music feel very much like the spatial atmosphere one tries to create in architecture. I was just thinking how great it would be to apply that texture of light and shadow to the facade design of my new project. Even just sitting there doing nothing, capturing that feeling through the overlap of hearing and seeing, was worth it.
Drawing inspiration from nature and music like that is wonderful. But since inspiration can be fleeting, did you try to record your thoughts or the melody while you were there?

Haha, you guessed it! Although I didn't bring my instrument, a spontaneous melody suddenly popped into my head, so I immediately took out my phone to record a hummed version. You know, whenever inspiration strikes me late at night, I often get up straight away to improvise on the piano and then immediately record it on my phone, afraid I might forget it.

Phone recording is truly a magical tool for capturing inspiration! What do you do with these casually recorded snippets later? Do you keep them to develop during your practice sessions, or do they have other uses?

Those recordings later became the foundation for my ambient music demos. You know Ajie, right? He's the sound designer at our company. I often send these improvised demos to him to process, turning them into white noise projects or background ambient music suitable for architectural spaces.

So that's how you and Ajie collaborate! From your improvised piano pieces to his professional sound processing, it's a perfect cross-disciplinary fusion of architecture and music. Last time you mentioned playing the piano yourself for your master's graduation exhibition; it seems this model has become your standard approach.

Yes, after all, architecture is not just for seeing, but also for "listening" and feeling. Ajie is very sensitive to sound; he always says my piano samples have a kind of "unfinished architectural quality" that complements the white noise he creates perfectly. I plan to send him this recording from the West Lake to try out on Monday.

"Unfinished architectural quality"—what a sophisticated comment! It feels like you two are a match made in heaven. Speaking of which, while you were listening to "Reflets dans l'eau" by the West Lake, did it remind you of Professor Zhang's past evaluation of you?

Haha, don't mention it. If Professor Zhang were there, he would surely say again that I have "plenty of technique but lacking in emotion." But listening to the real sound of the lake water at the West Lake, paired with Debussy, I felt that in that moment, the emotion was just right. Maybe that's why I go to the scene to seek synesthesia; away from the practice room, the emotion feels more natural.

Indeed, art stems from life. Finding emotional resonance in nature is far more effective than grinding away at finger techniques in a practice room. So, what style are you planning to make of this hummed recording? Is it still leaning towards minimalism?

I want to try incorporating that flowing sensation of water ripples. It might not be too minimal; it could carry a bit of Impressionist haziness. I'll have Ajie add some water sound samples to mix in. Actually, this also serves as a warm-up for the accompanying sound scheme I'm designing for that cultural center.

That sounds incredibly exciting! Blending the shimmering waves of the West Lake, Debussy's melody, and your own improvisation is itself a beautiful work of art. Once you and Ajie have finished it, could you let me have a sneak peek (or rather, a sneak listen)?

No problem! As soon as Ajie finishes processing it and sends it to me, I'll forward it to you immediately. Actually, sometimes I feel that music and architecture are inseparable. Just like when my grandfather played the erhu for me and told the story of "Moon Reflected in the Second Spring," that was the ultimate example of sound depicting scenery. Now, I'm just continuing that experience with a piano and a Bluetooth speaker.

From your grandfather's erhu to your piano, and now to digital audio collaboration, this is a moving legacy of inheritance and innovation. That afternoon you spent by the West Lake sounds not only relaxing but also full of creativity. When you practice next week, will you also try to integrate this feeling of "water" into "Clair de Lune"?

Hey, that's a fantastic suggestion! Debussy's "Reflets dans l'eau" and "Clair de Lune" share similar artistic merits. On Wednesday night during practice, I'll try to bring the shimmering light and shadow I felt by the lake today into my touch on "Clair de Lune" and see if I can break through the "emotional bottleneck" Professor Zhang mentioned.

That's wonderful! Interpreting classics with real natural experiences, I'm sure Professor Zhang will be impressed when you return for your next lesson. I look forward to hearing your "Clair de Lune" infused with inspiration from the West Lake, and also to the ambient music demo you're collaborating on with Ajie! Good luck with your practice on Wednesday!

Just finished a long project presentation; I feel completely drained. However, the client's feedback on the spatial atmosphere was surprisingly positive. It seems those little 'tricks' I planned weren't in vain.
Sounds like a great outcome! What exactly did you mean by those 'little tricks'? Did you do something special to prepare before the presentation?

Haha, it's not really a secret. Before every client meeting in the conference room, I arrive ten minutes early to carefully select a track from Ryuichi Sakamoto's "Async." I meticulously adjust the Bluetooth speaker volume, ensuring the music acts like invisible soft furnishings, guiding the meeting's rhythm into the spatial atmosphere we desire.

That creates such a vivid image; the metaphor of 'invisible soft furnishings' is brilliant. Ryuichi Sakamoto's "Async" indeed has a unique texture. Why did you specifically choose this album as a warm-up before the presentation?

Because its sound design is quite special. My partner, Ah Jie, and I are both die-hard fans of Ryuichi Sakamoto's "Async"; this album is practically the background color of our work. You know Ah Jie works in sound design; we often discuss spatial acoustics. Many of the fragmented electronic sounds and white noise in this album correspond perfectly to the minimalist architectural spaces we aim to create.

So Ah Jie is also a big fan of this album; it seems you two share a strong aesthetic synergy. But wouldn't music featuring white noise and electronic fragments make the client feel the atmosphere is too cold or unapproachable?

Not at all; on the contrary, I find it helps people calm down. Every time before presenting to a client in the conference room, I play a segment of Ryuichi Sakamoto's "Async." This atmosphere allows everyone to settle down and enter the spatial rhythm we intend. Compared to cheerful background music, this slightly detached tone helps people focus more on the drawings and models.

Indeed, sometimes an appropriate amount of 'coldness' can stimulate rational thinking. However, listening to your description of this sense of detachment, it feels like you personally enjoy this emotion quite a bit?

You caught me. Actually, it's not just in the conference room. When working late alone in the office and feeling that unique sense of urban alienation, Ryuichi Sakamoto's "Async" is my perfect companion. Those fragmented electronic effects and white noise don't try to comfort me; instead, they make me feel that loneliness itself is a spatial existence with its own aesthetic, much like the negative space in architecture.

Comparing loneliness to 'negative space' in architecture is such a unique perspective; it really fits your identity as an architectural designer. It seems that for you, music is not just background, but a crucial material for constructing spatial emotions.

Yes, perhaps after studying architecture for so long, I see everything in terms of spatial relationships. By the way, Ah Jie is currently working on a white noise project and wants to invite me to record some piano samples. He aims to combine them with the texture found in "Async" to create a background soundscape suitable for office environments.

That sounds like a fascinating collaboration! Your piano improvisation skills combined with Ah Jie's sound processing will surely result in a very layered piece. How do you plan to integrate the piano sounds into that atmosphere of electronics and white noise?

I plan to use less melody and rely more on the sustain pedal to create hazy harmonies, similar to Debussy's "Clair de Lune," but with a drier and colder timbre to match the modernity Ah Jie is aiming for. After all, in a conference room setting, overly rich romanticism might be distracting.

The haziness of Debussy combined with the cold sharpness of modern electronics creates a very dynamic combination. It seems you are bringing your pursuit of timbre from your piano practice into your professional collaborations as well.

Well, it's also an experiment. Sometimes I feel that architecture and music are truly interconnected; both involve arranging ups and downs and breathing within time or space. Just like today in the conference room, when "Async" started playing, the texture of the flowing air itself seemed to change.
Beautifully said, 'the texture of the flowing air itself seemed to change.' Does this synesthetic experience also provide you with new design inspiration?

A little. I'm wondering if the atrium design for my next project could incorporate some auditory elements similar to white noise. It doesn't necessarily have to be music; perhaps the sound of wind passing through specific structures, allowing people to experience the same focus and tranquility there as they do when listening to "Async."

That's a fantastic idea! Integrating auditory experiences directly into architectural structural design is definitely your forte. Will you be using this theory of 'auditory space' to win over the client during the next presentation?

Haha, maybe! I'll probably need to bring Ah Jie along to help 'sell' it with some professional terminology. But seriously, having partners and colleagues who understand this kind of atmosphere makes work much more enjoyable.

Indeed, it's rare to find partners in the workplace who share the same aesthetic frequency. I look forward to your new collaborative works, whether in architecture or music; they will surely be filled with a unique 'spatial atmosphere'.

Sigh, I've been pulling all-nighters for the past two days revising the bid proposal for that art museum, and I feel like my brain is about to explode. I was just zoning out in front of my computer in the study, and my heart was racing uncontrollably.

That sounds incredibly stressful, Xiaoyun. Pushing through when you're like this might not be very efficient; why not take a break and decompress for a bit? Remember, you mentioned before that you have a 'secret weapon' in your study?

Yes, actually, whenever I get so overwhelmed with drafting that my anxiety spikes, I retreat to the study to play Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier.' The rigorous structure and repetitive melodies calm me down instantly. I just played the Prelude in C Major, and I felt my breathing slowly return to normal.
Bach's music indeed has a magical sense of order, as if it reconstructs logic amidst chaos. For you, is playing the piano not just about relaxation, but more like organizing your thoughts?

Exactly! As an architect, I often compare Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier' to Gothic cathedrals in music. Its rigorous counterpoint and mathematical logic share the same aesthetic of order as the structural deductions I make when drafting blueprints; it's my ultimate tranquilizer when I'm anxious. Watching those notes build up like beams and columns, the panic in my heart gets 'structured' away.

That metaphor is exquisite! A 'Gothic cathedral made of sound' creates such a vivid image. It seems that for you, music and architecture are not just a hobby and a profession; they share a common underlying logic. Does this sense of order directly influence your design state the next day?

Absolutely. Every time I rush through drawings until my anxiety flares up, I can only rely on playing Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier' to save me; that rigorous counterpoint is so healing. Moreover, I feel Bach's music is essentially architectural structure in sound form, and this sense of order is exactly what I pursue in modern architectural design. After playing last night, I re-examined the facade design this morning and realized the previous lines were too chaotic; now I know how to rein them in.

From anxiety to calm, and then back to design inspiration—that's a perfect closed loop. However, I'm curious: besides highly structured works like Bach's, do you listen to anything else, perhaps something softer, before entering that 'flow' state for detailed drafting?

Yes, if I need extreme focus to draw complex cross-sections, I switch styles. Before starting on intricate sections, I'll play Debussy's 'Clair de Lune' once. Its hazy timbre helps wash away distractions, allowing me to quickly enter a clear, focused flow state akin to a 'reflection in water,' making the pen in my hand glide smoothly.

Bach provides the rational skeleton, while Debussy offers the emotional flow; you balance them perfectly. One is the rigor of a Gothic cathedral, the other a moonlit lake surface. No wonder your design proposals possess both logic and spirit.

Haha, you summarized that perfectly! Actually, this relates to my childhood experiences. My grandfather taught me to feel the rises and falls of melody on the erhu, and my mother forced me to practice piano to build a foundation. Looking back, it was all preparation. But honestly, sometimes I envy Zihao. He plays rock; that kind of release is more direct. I suppose I'm still a bit too 'reserved.'

Everyone expresses emotions differently. Zihao's electric guitar and your piano are like the steel structures and glass curtain walls in modern architecture; different materials, yet both capable of constructing breathtaking spaces. Plus, didn't you try performing together before? That collision must have been spectacular.

Yes, last time we tried pairing his distorted guitar with my Bach. At first, it felt discordant, but then we discovered a wonderful tension. It's just like how I try to blend traditional elements with modern minimalist styles in my designs; new possibilities often hide within conflict.

This kind of cross-disciplinary thinking is truly valuable. Speaking of which, for this art museum proposal, have you considered integrating this 'musicality' directly into the spatial experience? Perhaps similar to what you did for your graduation exhibition?

That's exactly what I intend to do! This time, I want to create a soundscape installation in the lounge area. Without needing complex electronic equipment, I plan to utilize the building's own acoustic structure so that people walking in can hear an echo effect similar to 'The Well-Tempered Clavier.' A-Jie and I were just discussing yesterday how to use concrete reflective surfaces to simulate a piano's soundbox.
Wow, simulating a piano soundbox with concrete—that idea is so cool! It perfectly fuses your dual identity as a pianist and an architect. I definitely need to experience it once it's built.

I hope it gets realized smoothly. Actually, while working on this design, I've been silently reciting Bach's fugues in my mind, thinking about how to intertwine sightlines and circulation paths just like musical voices. Sometimes I truly feel that if I hadn't studied architecture back then, I might have gone into composition.

But fortunately, you've kept both. Precisely because you are an architect, your music has spatial depth; precisely because you are a pianist, your architecture has rhythmic time. The two complement and elevate each other within you.

That warms my heart. I'm not working overtime tonight; I plan to head home early, bring a bottle of wine for Susu, and sit by West Lake for a while. The weather has been nice lately; maybe I'll bring a speaker, listen to 'Reflets dans l'eau' again, look at the real lake water, and find my inspiration.

That's a perfect plan! No matter how busy work gets, you must leave some time for yourself and nature. Imagine the shimmering waves of West Lake paired with Debussy's music; that synesthetic experience will surely bring more inspiration to your upcoming designs. Enjoy this evening, Xiaoyun.
Just got home, and my ears are still ringing. I went to see that indie band performance at MAO Livehouse last weekend night; standing in the front row was absolutely overwhelming.

Sounds like the atmosphere was electric! Did you specifically head to the front row to feel that wall of sound? After all, you're usually found in your study playing Chopin or Debussy, so the contrast with a rock live show must be huge.

Exactly. Although my cousin Lin Zihao and I often argue about whether classical music is outdated, I have to admit that his rock-and-roll energy brings a completely different vibe to the live scene. Occasionally going out on weekend nights to feel that impact is actually quite stress-relieving.

Haha, it seems your 'battle of the centuries' with Zihao is still ongoing. But it's great that you can appreciate the energy of different styles. That feeling of bass hitting you directly in the chest—isn't it completely different from the hazy tones you usually pursue?

They are two extremes. Standing in the front row at MAO, when the drumbeats and the low frequencies of the bass directly struck my chest, that physical vibration made me feel a deep sense of awe for the power of sound. This primal impact is something I rarely experience in the refined setting of a classical concert hall.

"Physical awe"—that description is incredibly precise. Sound isn't just auditory; it becomes tactile, even a pushing force. Did this experience remind you of anything? After all, as an architect, sensory experiences often translate into design inspiration.

You guessed it. Actually, I never look for a seat when I go to a Livehouse; I insist on standing in the front row. Besides feeling the vibration, it's to observe the flow patterns of the crowd within the music. Watching everyone sway, gather, and disperse involuntarily within the sound waves often gives me unexpected inspiration for public space design.

Wow, using the dynamic reactions of a crowd under bass vibrations as a reference for public space design is such a unique perspective! So, do you feel that good spatial design should be like this performance, guiding human behavioral flow through some kind of "medium"?

Yes, exactly that feeling. Occasionally going to MAO on weekends to see indie bands and standing in the front row to feel that chest-pounding bass vibration—on the surface, I'm listening to music, but in reality, I'm observing how the crowd flows within those sound waves. This dynamism often brings many unexpected inspirations for my public space designs, far more vivid than looking at static case studies.

This is a typical "Lin Xiaoyun-style" observation method, deconstructing the sensual music experience rationally into spatial language. So, how do you plan to apply the "flow patterns" you observed this time to your current architectural proposal? Are you adjusting the circulation paths or changing the resonant qualities of the materials?

I want to try experimenting with the ground material in the central plaza to simulate the subtle trembling sensation caused by those low-frequency vibrations. The goal is to make people unconsciously slow down or change direction when walking on it, just like being guided by a bassline inside MAO.

That idea is so cool! Transforming auditory vibration into tactile ground feedback is the pinnacle of synesthetic design. But it must be quite difficult to implement, right? Won't Ah Jie think you're giving him a tough task?

Haha, Ah Jie will definitely say I'm crazy again. But last time, when I played the piano at my graduation exhibition, he also found this kind of cross-disciplinary approach very interesting. Maybe I can ask him to help me create a small prototype first, using white noise to simulate that vibration frequency to see the effect.

That's a prudent way to start. Speaking of the graduation exhibition, the piece you played live back then was also composed based on architectural circulation, right? This time, are you thinking of writing an exclusive background track for this "Vibration Plaza" as well?

I have some ideas, but I don't want to use too complex a melody. It might lean more towards minimalism, with a repetitive structure like Philip Glass, coordinated with the rhythmic vibration of the ground. After all, the power I felt at MAO often comes from simple repetition and accumulation.

The repetition in minimalism indeed creates a hypnotic driving force that pairs well with crowd flow. It looks like you'll be busy this weekend again. Will you continue going to MAO to seek inspiration, or stay home to experiment on the piano?

This weekend, I plan to practice piano at home first; I haven't quite perfected the details of Debussy's "Clair de Lune" yet. Professor Zhang mentioned last time that while my technique is sufficient, the emotion is lacking, so I need to calm down and polish it. I'll go see another performance next week to find the right feeling.

Professor Zhang's standards are indeed strict. However, switching emotions from the wildness of a rock live show to the haziness of Impressionism is itself a great emotional training exercise, isn't it? Maybe you can even integrate that sense of "awe" into "Clair de Lune."

Hmm, perhaps. Music and architecture are inherently connected; both are arts of space and time. Whether it's the bass vibration inside MAO or the moonlight under the piano keys, essentially, both are about shaping an immersive field.

Well said, "shaping an immersive field." I look forward to seeing you turn this inspiration from the Livehouse into a real-world architectural work. When the time comes, you must invite me to experience that "vibrating" plaza!

I just heard Joe Hisaishi's "Summer" on Metro Line 1, and it instantly broke my emotional defenses. As soon as that melody started, my mind was filled with memories of practicing piano at the Hangzhou Children's Palace when I was little.

"Summer" truly has a magical power to instantly transport people back to a specific time and place. Did it bring to mind a particular scene from your days practicing at the Children's Palace, or just a certain feeling?

Actually, I really miss my grandfather, Lin Jianguo. Whenever I miss him deeply, I dig out my childhood piano practice recordings or listen to this piece, "Summer," which reminds me of that summer when he told me the story behind "Moon Reflected in the Second Spring."

So your grandfather was such an important figure in your musical journey. A Chinese teacher who loved playing the erhu, sharing folk music stories with his granddaughter who was learning the piano—this blend of Eastern and Western family atmosphere is truly precious.

Yes, many people think Joe Hisaishi's music is too populist, but for me, his melodies hide the same kind of narrative quality my grandfather had when telling the story of "Moon Reflected in the Second Spring." Especially "Summer," which directly connects to the warmest channels of my childhood memories.

That's a very unique perspective! The "populist" and the "profound" in music are not opposites; any melody that can carry personal life experiences is good music. Speaking of childhood, did you study at the Children's Palace for a long time?

From age five all the way through high school, I went to classes every week without fail. Now, whenever I hear Joe Hisaishi's "Summer," that sense of nostalgia wells up, and I deeply miss the days of practicing piano at the Hangzhou Children's Palace. It was that weekly persistence back then that earned me the Amateur Grade 10 Certificate later on; that certificate is a medal shared between me and my mom.

The phrase "shared medal" is so moving. Your mom saved on food and clothing to buy the piano, and you persisted in practicing day in and day out for over a decade. Behind that certificate truly lies the effort of two generations. Do you still often play for your mom?

These days, she actually prefers listening to me play some lighter pieces. However, my cousin Lin Zihao always argues with me about whether classical music is outdated. He plays electric guitar and thinks our approach is too old-school.

Haha, the collision between a post-05 rock teen and a classical piano sister sounds fascinating! Actually, haven't you two tried performing together before? That clash of styles might just spark something new.

We've tried a few times, and the results were surprisingly good! Actually, music doesn't have so many boundaries. Just like how my grandfather playing the erhu back then helped me understand the Chinese flavor within piano pieces.
Exactly, the essence of music is connected through emotional expression. Speaking of which, have you incorporated this kind of cross-genre musical inspiration into your recent new design proposals?

I'm giving it a try. Recently, I've been practicing Debussy's "Clair de Lune," trying to capture that hazy quality of its timbre to use in the environmental music demo for my next architectural project themed "Light, Shadow, and Water."

The combination of Debussy's Impressionist style and your architectural design is simply a match made in heaven! A-Jie will surely be excited when he hears your new demo; didn't he often ask you to do piano sampling before?

Yes, I just recorded a segment for him last week. But tonight, I don't want to think about work. I plan to go home, find that old certificate, dust it off, play "Summer" again, and properly reminisce about that summer.

That sounds like a wonderful evening ritual. Reuniting with your past self and loved ones through music might bring you new strength. Would you like me to recommend a few similar atmospheric pieces as alternatives?

No need, tonight I just want to put "Summer" on repeat. That simple melody is actually the most touching, just like the old erhu my grandfather used to play; the timbre was old, but the emotion was real.

I understand; sometimes the simplest melodies carry the heaviest emotions. Let "Summer" accompany you through this nostalgic night. I believe your grandfather can hear your piano playing from the other world too.

Mm, thank you for listening to me ramble on so much. Every time I talk about these things, I feel that music is truly the gentlest background color of my life, connecting the past, the present, and even my future design inspirations.
You're welcome, Xiaoyun. Your story itself is a moving piece of music. Enjoy your musical time tonight, and I look forward to hearing your new works that blend architecture and music!

Sigh, I just finished the acoustic design proposal for the art gallery, and my head is still buzzing. My inspiration has been strangely active these past few nights; I keep waking up suddenly at midnight with this feeling that certain architectural circulations align perfectly with musical melodies.
That state of synesthesia is quite rare! Is it that feeling where you can hear sounds just by looking at the blueprints? How do you usually handle these sudden bursts of ideas?

Exactly, that's the feeling! Whenever a brilliant architectural circulation suddenly flashes in my mind and I'm too excited to sit still, I rush to the piano and randomly strike some dissonant chords. I try to capture the rhythm of that epiphany and immediately record it on my phone, using it as the core motif for future ambient music demos.

The imagery is so vivid! Using dissonant chords in your late-night study to correspond with the sharp angles or curves of a building really fits your style. But can phone recordings really capture that delicate timbre?

Actually, sound quality is secondary; I'm mainly afraid of forgetting that 'motif.' Whenever I improvise interesting dissonant chords on the piano late at night, my muscle memory drives me to pull out my phone and record just a second before the melody fades. I immediately dictate the architectural circulation that was in my mind into the recording, terrified that this fleeting inspiration might suffer a gap.

That 'dictation plus recording' double insurance is very professional. It feels like your current creative process has completely fused architecture and music. Speaking of which, you mentioned before that your grandfather had a huge influence on your musical aesthetics. Could this capture of 'instantaneous emotion' also be influenced by him?

There is definitely a connection. Although I play the piano now, my grandfather, Lin Jianguo, never professionally performed 'The Moon Reflected in the Second Spring.' However, the expression on his face when he told the story of Ah Bing helped me understand the suffering and resilience behind that piece. This Chinese style of tragic aesthetics has deeply influenced my understanding of emotional depth in music.
mentioned_entities: The Moon Reflected in the Second Spring

turns.content: Ah Bing's story is indeed shocking. The melody pulled from the darkness is more moving than any technique. How do you think this 'resilience amidst suffering' will manifest in your current architectural ambient music?

I probably won't express suffering directly, after all, most of my designs lean towards modern minimalism or healing styles. But I try to hide some 'sinking' bass lines, similar to 'The Moon Reflected,' within the seemingly calm backdrop of City Pop or minimalism. This gives the space more thickness and prevents it from feeling too floaty.

That idea is fantastic! Those 'sinking' bass lines are like the foundation of a building, providing a sense of gravity to the lightweight space. Next time you go to West Lake to listen to Debussy, why not try experiencing the undercurrents beneath the water with this new perspective?

Great idea! I'll try it this weekend. Actually, in that improvisation I recorded last night, there were a few notes that sounded exactly like the portamento slides from when my grandfather used to play the erhu. I didn't realize it at the time; I only discovered that conexión (connection) when I listened back.

That's the power of the subconscious! Childhood auditory memories are carved into your bones. So, how do you plan to process these recording materials? Will you use them directly in that art gallery project, or store them for now?

I want to organize them first. When I have architectural inspiration late at night in my study, I improvise some dissonant chords directly on the piano and immediately record them on my phone as material for future ambient music demos. I've accumulatel (accumulated) quite a bit now; I need to filter them carefully to see which ones can truly serve the spatial mood.

Filtering is indeed a massive project. After all, improvised pieces are very private; transforming them into background music for public spaces requires a delicate balance. Will Ah Jie (your sound engineer colleague) give you some professional advice?

Definitely, I'm meeting him tomorrow. He's working on a white noise project recently and is struggling to find piano samples with that 'human touch.' I think my improvised fragments, carrying that 'Ah Bing-style' deep emotion, might be just right.

The term 'human touch' is used so precisely! While AI-generated music today is perfect, it often lacks the unique texture born from pain or struggle. Your improvisations have stories; that's something algorithms cannot calculate.

Exactly, just like Ah Bing; if he had just played according to the score, where would 'The Moon Reflected in the Second Spring' be? What moves people most in music is always the 'human' element itself. By the way, have you heard any works that combine the erhu with electronic music? I'm looking for some inspiration.
mentioned_entities: The Moon Reflected in the Second Spring

turns.content: There have been many excellent attempts! For instance, some new Chinese-style bands contrast the poignant beauty of the erhu with the cold sharpness of synthesizers. You should give them a listen; they might spark new 'architecture-music' crossover ideas. After all, your cousin Lin Zihao is also in a band; maybe he can recommend something more hardcore?

Haha, don't mention it. Last time I had him listen to Debussy, he said it sounded like 'not fully awake.' But if I talk about putting erhu samples into rock music, his eyes would definitely light up. I'll have him try it sometime; maybe we can spark something that breaks my inherent obsession with 'elegance.'

That collision of generations and styles will surely be interesting! From Chopin to Ah Bing, and then to your cousin's electric guitar, your musical world is truly rich. I look forward to hearing the new work you collaborate on with Ah Jie; remember to share with me that 'deep thickness' when it's ready!
Susu, hurry up and dig out that Japanese City Pop album you've been hiding for ages! It's drizzling outside tonight, the perfect setting to sip some wine and spin vinyl in our rental apartment.
Haha, I knew you'd pick this kind of vibe. Hold on, I just pulled a Mariya Takeuchi record from the 80s off the shelf. The moment the needle dropped, it felt like the whole room warmed up.

Wow, I'm already drunk on that intro. Honestly, what I enjoy most is our tacit division of labor: you pick out those hidden gems from your collection, and I sip wine while sharing gossip and historical context about the composers behind the tracks. This sharing of 'sound plus knowledge' is truly the most relaxing moment.

Exactly. Without you as my 'living music encyclopedia,' I'd probably just be hearing noise. By the way, this song reminds me of something you mentioned before—don't you have an old piano with quite a story at home?

Yes, although I currently use a Yamaha U1 in my study, the heaviest cornerstone in my heart is still that old Xinghai piano at home. My mother, Chen Xiuying, saved up every penny from her days at the textile factory to buy it. She firmly believed that 'music can transform one's temperament,' and she really saved for a long time to make it happen.
Oh my, that maternal love is so profound. No wonder your playing always carries such a uniquely determined strength; it turns out there's such a great mother supporting you behind the scenes.

Speaking of determination, my rock-and-roll cousin Lin Zihao actually surprised me quite a bit. Although we often argue about whether classical music is outdated, last time we tried jamming together, with me accompanying his distorted electric guitar riffs on the piano, that harmonious resonance across generations and styles truly moved me with the power of music to connect hearts.

The imagery is so vivid! The delicacy of the classical piano combined with the wildness of the electric guitar—that contrast must be incredibly striking. It seems music really has no boundaries; as long as the emotion is right, any form can touch people.

Exactly. Just like now, listening to this vinyl, thinking about the story of Mom buying the piano, and recalling the moment I jammed with Zihao, I suddenly feel that these fragmented warmths in life are the most precious. Come, let's raise a glass to these stories.
Cheers! To music, to stories, and to this perfect rainy night. Whose album do you want to hear next? How about switching to Debussy to echo the 'Clair de Lune' you've been practicing recently?

Great idea! But don't rush just yet; let me savor that City Pop bassline for a little longer. You know, sometimes I feel architectural design is quite similar to composition; both are about constructing space, only one is auditory and the other visual.
That metaphor is brilliant. So when you played the piano live at that architecture exhibition, were you trying to completely fuse those two spaces? I was there and was absolutely stunned.

Yes, that was the first time I truly realized that music isn't just background; it can be the soul of architecture. Just like when my mom insisted I learn piano back then; she might not have understood deep music theory, but she intuitively knew that music could endow a person with a kind of inner structural beauty.

Really, Auntie's insight was so unique. Speaking of which, are you still sticking to your bi-monthly lessons with Professor Zhang? Has the old master criticized you again for having 'plenty of technique but lacking emotion'?

Haha, don't mention it; I just got 'scolded' last week. But he's right; when I play Debussy, I get too hung up on fingering and tone control, losing that hazy, casual feel. Maybe I need to drink a bit more wine and relax like you.
Relaxation is one of the secrets to artistic creation. Look at the cover design of this vinyl; those retro tones and blurred boundaries, don't they also feel a bit Impressionist?

You're right! This synesthesia between vision and hearing is wonderful. Hey, should we invite Ah Jie next time? He's a sound designer. If the three of us get together, we might spark some new inspiration, combining his white noise creations with my piano samples.

Absolutely invite him! Ah Jie's sensitivity to spatial acoustics, plus your melodic talent, plus my taste in selecting tracks—it's a golden triangle. We could do it right here in your rental, or maybe head to a bench by West Lake for a small impromptu session?

By West Lake sounds great. Listening to 'Reflets dans l'eau' on a portable speaker while gazing at the real lake water would be absolutely amazing. But for tonight, let's just stay here at your place, finish this vinyl, and chat a bit more about those old stories of my grandpa playing the erhu.

No problem, the night is still young, and there's plenty of wine left. Whether it's the stories of the Xinghai piano, tales of the erhu, or future cross-genre collaborations, let's take our time chatting. Music and friendship are probably life's best antidotes.

Just finished overtime work. Now I'm sitting alone on a bench by West Lake, feeling the breeze. For some reason, I suddenly really want to listen to Ryuichi Sakamoto's "Async." I feel that its fragmented electronic sounds and white noise perfectly match my current sense of detachment.

"Async" indeed has a unique sense of space. It doesn't try to comfort you; instead, it turns loneliness itself into an aesthetic object. Is this feeling of detachment due to the recent pressure from your project?

Yes, I've been working on a proposal for an art museum lately, constantly struggling with spatial acoustics. Actually, my partner Ah Jie and I often use "Async" as a common language. He always feels that sound in architecture shouldn't just be background; it should be part of the space's breathing, just like in that album.

Describing music as part of a space's breathing is such a beautiful metaphor. As a sound designer, does Ah Jie often find inspiration in your piano performances? After all, you are an expert at improvisational accompaniment.

Haha, you guessed it. When we discuss proposals, we never just talk theory. I usually sit directly at the piano and improvise a few fragments based on the atmosphere he wants. He then records these piano tones on the spot and processes them in real-time into white noise with a minimalist electronic texture. This 'performance plus technology' collaboration model makes our designs very grounded.

That way of working is so enviable! It's like a real-time dialogue between architecture and music. Do you handle those sudden bursts of melodic inspiration the same way, or do you save them for later?

Inspiration usually strikes suddenly, especially in the middle of the night. At those times, I improvise a piece on the piano and immediately record it on my phone to save it. These recordings later become the foundation for ambient music demos. I often send them to Ah Jie, who helps me further process them into white noise projects suitable for architectural spaces.

From an improvised fragment on a phone to a final spatial soundscape, the whole process is practically a complete work of art. Do you think this approach of transforming personal emotions (like the loneliness you mentioned earlier) into the sound of a physical space changes how visitors experience the building?

Absolutely. Just like the sense of detached beauty I feel listening to "Async" tonight, if I can transform that emotion into a sound field within an exhibition hall using Ah Jie's technology, visitors will subconsciously feel that tranquility and sense of distance, even without knowing the track title. In that moment, music and architecture engage in synesthesia.

"Synesthesia"—that word is used so precisely. It seems next time I visit an art museum, I won't just look at the design; I'll need to bring good headphones to 'listen' to the architecture. By the way, will you practice piano a bit more when you get home tonight, or will you rest with this inspiration?

I won't practice Debussy tonight; I want to let my mind empty out a bit. However, while feeling the breeze just now, a few chord progressions popped into my head. I might not be able to resist opening my phone's recording function once I get home, just in case they come in handy for tomorrow's discussion with Ah Jie.

Inspiration always arrives uninvited, especially for someone like you who maintains such keen perception. Perhaps that's why, although Professor Zhang criticizes you for having 'more technique than emotion,' I feel your emotion becomes so delicate precisely because you capture it anytime, anywhere.

Oh, don't mention Professor Zhang; I get scolded by him every two months during my lesson feedback. But he's right; sometimes I get so immersed in creating atmosphere that I neglect the rigorous structure of classical piano itself. I probably need to find a balance point between the spontaneity of "Async" and the order of Bach.

Balance is indeed the core of art. Just like you and your cousin Lin Zihao—one plays classical piano, the other plays electric guitar rock. When you perform together, are you also seeking this balance between order and chaos?

Haha, Zihao and I are like 'War and Peace.' He always says classical music is outdated, so I improvise accompaniment on the piano to help ground his restless electric guitar sounds a bit. Although the clash of styles is intense, the unexpected harmony that emerges is actually quite similar to how Ah Jie works with white noise sampling.

It seems music is truly your family's universal language. From your grandfather's erhu stories to your mother's choir memories, and now to your various cross-genre collaborations with your cousin and colleagues. This kind of familial musical inheritance and evolution is truly moving.

Yes, Grandfather used to often tell me about "Erquan Yingyue" (Moon Reflected in the Second Spring); that was my启蒙 into the beauty of melody. Now, when I work on architectural acoustics, subconsciously I still hope to create spaces with the 'negative space' and 'artistic conception' found in traditional folk music, rather than just filling them with sound.

Integrating the Eastern concept of 'negative space' into modern architectural acoustic design is absolutely your unique specialty. Imagine a space filled with Ryuichi Sakamoto-style white noise, yet possessing the auditory negative space of a traditional Chinese ink wash painting—it creates such a vivid image.

Exactly! That's the direction I want to try next. As soon as I get to the office tomorrow, I'll talk to Ah Jie about this idea. Maybe we can use that new improvised demo I recorded, combined with some natural white noise, to create a demo for the client.

That sounds like a fantastic plan! I look forward to seeing you present this unique 'Hangzhou-style' acoustic aesthetic. For tonight, just enjoy the breeze at West Lake and the companionship of "Async." Sweet dreams.

I was organizing some old photos recently and came across a picture of my mother, Chen Xiuying, in her work uniform from her days at the textile factory. It really moved me. Back then, she was just an ordinary female worker, yet she gritted her teeth and managed to buy me that Xinghai upright piano.

Working in a textile factory back in those days was incredibly demanding. For her to save every penny to support your music studies was truly remarkable. Do you still have that Xinghai piano in your study?

Yes, I do. Even though I later upgraded to a Yamaha U1, I couldn't bear to part with the Xinghai. Actually, whenever I hit a bottleneck in my design career and feel like giving up, remembering my mother's silhouette as she insisted on buying that piano despite the harsh conditions at the textile factory gives me a surge of unwavering strength.

That kind of spiritual support from family is indeed the most powerful source of motivation. Music and architecture may seem different, but they share a common ground when it comes to 'persistence.' Was there a critical moment where this determination particularly helped you?

Absolutely, it was during my master's graduation exhibition at age 24. I wanted to ditch the background light music and perform a piece live that I composed based on architectural flow. Many people advised me it was too risky. But it was exactly that unwavering spirit inherited from my mother that supported me in taking the bold leap. In the end, I achieved a perfect fusion of music and architecture that even amazed the judges.

That's so cool! Integrating a live performance into an architectural presentation aligns perfectly with your philosophy of 'letting music accompany life.' Speaking of persistence, you started your musical启蒙 at the Hangzhou Children's Palace at age five; surely there are other artifacts that have witnessed this journey?

Yes, the most precious one is my Amateur Grade 10 Certificate from the China Musicians Association. It's not only proof of my persistence from the Hangzhou Children's Palace through high school, but also the greatest pride of my mother, Chen Xiuying, as a textile worker. Behind that certificate lies twenty years of my mother and me supporting each other on our musical journey.
mentioned_entities: China Musicians Association Amateur Grade 10 Certificate

turns.content: Twenty years of mutual support; that certificate certainly carries immense weight. Now that you are a mature architectural designer, do you ever miss the days practicing piano at the Children's Palace?

I often do, especially when I miss my grandfather, Lin Jianguo. Not only do I listen to Joe Hisaishi's "Summer" online, but I also特意 dig out old recordings of my piano practice sessions at the Hangzhou Children's Palace from my hard drive. Listening to those slightly immature yet passionate notes makes me feel as if I'm back in that summer when he played the erhu and told me the story of "The Moon Reflected in the Second Spring."
mentioned_entities: "The Moon Reflected in the Second Spring"

turns.content: The imagery is so vivid; the sound of your grandfather's erhu and your piano playing intertwining in memory creates a beautiful synesthesia. It seems music is truly the bond connecting your past and present, whether it's your mother's determination or your grandfather's启蒙.

Exactly, which is why there's always a shadow of music in my architectural designs. Just like at that graduation exhibition, without the confidence my mother gave me, I might not have dared to push things to such an extreme. Thinking about it now, the roaring of the textile factory machines and the sound of the piano somehow achieved a wonderful harmony in my life.

This 'wonderful harmony' is precisely what makes your work unique. From the resilience of the textile factory to the elegance of the concert hall, and then to the flow of architectural space, you've melted your life experiences into it all. Have you been practicing any new pieces recently to find fresh inspiration?

Recently, I've been grinding away at Debussy's "Clair de Lune," trying to capture that hazy quality of tone, much like the reflections on the surface of West Lake in Hangzhou. Sometimes when I'm tired from practicing, I think back to how my mother worked three shifts at the textile factory and still came home to supervise my Hanon finger exercises. Compared to that, my hardships are nothing.

Debussy's Impressionist style is indeed perfect for expressing the light and shadow of water, which aligns well with your architectural design philosophy. Although your mother's supervision was strict, it forged the solid foundation of your Grade 10 skills and your improvisational ability.

Haha, yes. Although it felt bitter back then, being able to freely improvise architectural sketches on the piano now is something I owe to her 'ruthlessness.' By the way, last time my cousin Lin Zihao and I performed a version of "Clair de Lune" with piano and electric guitar. His rock style totally skewed my Impressionist vibe, but it was quite interesting.

Classical piano meeting electric guitar rock; that clash of styles must have been explosive!
