It seems your family's musical atmosphere is incredibly inclusive, ranging from your grandfather's erhu and your mother's choral singing to your classical training and your cousin's rock. It's like a mini music festival.

Exactly. In our family, music has never been something lofty; it's simply part of life. As my mother often says, music can change one's temperament, but I feel it connects emotions even more. Whether it's that Grade 10 certificate or the piano sounds at the graduation exhibition, they are all vessels of love.

Well said; defining it as a 'vessel of love' is very precise. Your experience proves that even without becoming a professional musician, music can still be the strongest backing for your career and life. Do you have any new creative plans coming up?

I plan to compose a demo of ambient music for my next community center design proposal. I want to incorporate some folk music elements I heard at the Children's Palace when I was young, as a tribute to my grandfather. I hope that, just like at the graduation exhibition, the sonic narrative of the space will be even more moving.

Integrating folk music elements into a modern architectural soundscape is a fantastic idea! I believe that with the determination your mother gave you and the melodic sense your grandfather bestowed upon you, this new project will be outstanding. I look forward to hearing your new work!

I've been rushing through drawings all week; my head feels like it's about to explode. On the Line 1 subway just now, I listened to Philip Glass the entire way. It feels like only those constantly repeating melodies can help me calm down.

Looks like you're pulling another all-nighter for that new project? Philip Glass is indeed a miracle worker for relieving anxiety, especially with his rigorous sense of structure. Did it remind you of Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier'?

A bit like that, but not exactly. Actually, looking at his melodies that constantly repeat with subtle variations, they resemble the modern building facade design I'm working on right now. Both rely on the repetition of elements to construct a grand rhythm.

That analogy is brilliant! Translating auditory minimalism into the visual language of facades, creating a sense of space through the infinite replication and subtle variation of a single element—that really has the style you had when you graduated with your master's degree.

Haha, you caught me. Speaking of graduation, I've been missing that state of mind lately. I recall my master's thesis exhibition, where I ditched the background light music and personally played a piano piece based on fluid lines for my architectural proposal. Seeing the spark in the eyes of the judges and the audience, that sense of achievement from the perfect fusion of architecture and music was the most fulfilling moment of my career.

Susu mentioned that exhibition to me; she said the entire hall fell silent, with only your piano playing and the architecture conversing. Those moments are truly irreplaceable. In your current design work, do you still preserve this ritual of 'live performance'?

It's hard to find opportunities like that at the office usually, but I go looking for inspiration during the conceptualization phase. For instance, on weekends with good weather, I specifically bring a portable Bluetooth speaker to sit on a bench by West Lake, playing Debussy's 'Reflets dans l'eau'. Then I do nothing but stare at the real shimmering lake water, trying to capture that Impressionist texture of light and shadow amidst the overlap of visual and auditory senses for my facade designs.

Listening to 'Reflets dans l'eau' by West Lake while watching the real ripples—that synesthetic experience is truly romantic and fits your love for Impressionist aesthetics perfectly. Did Debussy's hazy timbre also give you inspiration for handling the reflection of light on building materials?

Exactly! Especially that feeling of light shattering on the water surface; I want to simulate it using perforated aluminum panels. By the way, Ajie is coming over tonight to ask me to record some piano samples for his new white noise project. I guess we'll be tossing and turning until late again.

Collaborating with Ajie always sparks interesting ideas, after all, he understands acoustics while you understand space. But don't overwork yourself. Remember, besides your practice sessions on Wednesdays and Fridays, leave some time to just zone out. Inspiration often comes from moments of relaxation.

Don't worry, I'll balance work and rest. Actually, after talking to you about all that, I feel much calmer inside. It feels like I've recharged and I'm ready to go back and keep polishing those facade details.
That's great! I look forward to seeing your new work. Maybe before our next trip to MAO Livehouse, I'll get to hear you share some new design insights again. Keep it up, great architect!

Thanks for the good wishes! By the way, last time Zihao insisted on dragging me to use his electric guitar to score my minimalist facade proposal. He said he wanted to create a 'dialogue between classical and rock.' Just thinking about it makes the mental image so beautiful I dare not look.

Haha, Zihao, that rock-and-roll kid, is always full of whimsical ideas. But maybe this clash of styles could actually bring you unexpected inspiration? Just like when you had the erhu and piano converse back in the day.
True, art is interconnected after all. Anyway, I'm off to the study to try tapping out the rhythm of the aluminum panel arrangement I just thought of on the keys to get a feel for it.
Great idea, let your Yamaha U1 participate in some 'architectural design' too. If you record any wonderful snippets, remember to share them with me.
No problem. If it turns out well, next time I come to your place I'll play it for you directly on the vinyl record player, and we can have Susu come over to critique it too.
That would be fantastic; I'm already looking forward to it. The combination of the three of you—an architect, an illustrator, and a music lover—is simply a perfect artistic iron triangle.
Haha, you're too kind. Well, I'd better get back to work. Talk later!
Alright, go catch your inspiration. Talk later!

Sigh, rushing two proposals this week has me completely overwhelmed. I sat in my study for ages earlier, just staring at my Yamaha U1 and hi-fi speakers. It's usually my 'sanctuary,' but today it felt incredibly suffocating.

Sounds like a classic case of 'designer anxiety' kicking in. Isn't that corner usually your favorite spot when inspiration strikes? How did it become a source of stress today? Maybe the lighting or the atmosphere was off?

It was probably just too bright. I'm used to turning off the main lights and leaving only that desk lamp on. You know, once anxiety flares up, I need that ritual of focusing on the mechanical motion of my fingers under limited light. It feels like the only way to pull my chaotic thoughts back into order.

That 'focus in the dark' is indeed very effective. Did you try it tonight, or did just sitting there make your fingers feel stiff? What piece do you usually choose for 'emergency relief' in times like these?

I just played Bach's 'The Well-Tempered Clavier' for half an hour. Honestly, when rushing drawings makes my heart race, the airtight structure of counterpoint, or the repetitive patterns in Philip Glass, act just like an architectural skeleton. They instantly prop up my emotions before they fall apart.

Using the logical structure of music to combat real-world chaos fits your architect's mindset perfectly. Speaking of skeletons and support, your current state is quite similar to when you practiced piano as a child; both times you've been relying on sheer 'persistence' to hold on.

Yeah, touching the keys of this Yamaha U1 just now, I suddenly missed the old Xinghai piano my mother, Chen Xiuying, gritted her teeth to buy at the textile factory all those years ago. Even though I don't play it anymore, that persistence has stayed with me. Back then, she scrimped and saved just so I could have an instrument to touch.

That old Xinghai carried so much maternal love and expectation. From the Xinghai of the past to the Yamaha U1 of today, while the tone and touch have upgraded, the core spirit of 'persisting for the sake of passion' seems to have never changed; it has just taken a different form to accompany you.

Exactly. Although this Yamaha U1 in the corner of my study has a clearer tone, better suited for exploring the hazy textures of Debussy, every time I sit here to practice, I still think of that old Xinghai. It feels like every keystroke now is an echo of that devotion from years ago.

That dialogue across time is truly moving. Perhaps tonight's anxiety stems from your desire to achieve a certain perfection in your current work, just as your mother hoped for your perfection back then? Relax a bit; like architecture, music sometimes needs 'negative space' too.

You're right; I might be trying to cram too much into this proposal. Just like playing Debussy, forcing it too hard loses that floating sensation of moonlight on water. I need to learn to handle my emotions and notes the same way I handle light and shadow in architecture.
That's the idea. Since you've already found order through Bach, why not try a little improvisation next? Don't worry about form; just treat it like sketching for this proposal, purely for yourself.

Great idea. Actually, I was just wondering if I could turn the circulation diagram of this proposal into a melody. As you know, I did exactly that for my master's graduation exhibition, and the result was quite good. Maybe it's time to let architecture and music 'resonate' once again.

That would be fantastic! Your study is practically a natural laboratory. The night view of West Lake outside the window, the Yamaha and hi-fi speakers inside, plus you, this 'dual-practicing' architect-musician—the image alone feels so textured.

Haha, don't flatter me to death. But seriously, in moments like these, I'm grateful I didn't give up the piano. If I had listened to my cousin back then and gone into rock, I'd probably be screaming my lungs out in a Livehouse now, instead of sorting out my thoughts with notes here.

Rock has its own catharsis, and classical has its introspection; both have their merits. But for you, clearly this introspective self-healing is more critical. How long do you plan to play? Should we record it and send it to Ah Jie? It might provide some new material for his white noise project.

Sure thing. Ah Jie complained last time that his ambient sounds lacked a bit of 'human touch.' I'll start a recording right now and dim that desk lamp in the study a bit; that semi-lit, semi-dark atmosphere is perfect for capturing those elusive melodies.

Perfect. Looking forward to hearing your new piece. Remember, don't strive for perfection. Just like your mother thought when she bought that Xinghai back then: as long as you are playing, as long as the music is sounding, it is the most valuable moment.

Mm, I feel much better inside. Looking at this Yamaha U1, it no longer feels like a cold instrument, but more like an old friend who has accompanied me through countless nights like tonight. Alright, I'm going to start. I'll send you a preview before I say goodnight.
Okay, I'll be waiting anytime. Enjoy your musical moment. May tonight's melody soothe all your anxiety. Sweet dreams, Designer Lin.

Morning! Just finished breakfast and staring blankly at my computer. The weather in Hangzhou today is so stifling it's hard to breathe; I absolutely don't feel like picking up my pen to draft anything.
Morning! This sauna-like weather really makes one irritable, especially when doing design work that requires a calm mind. How do you usually adjust your state in situations like this?

Actually, I have a fixed 'boot-up ritual.' Every morning at 7:30, no matter how busy I am, I eat a simple breakfast while listening to BBC Radio 3 in the background, casually flipping through that day's architectural blueprints. This dual input of visual and auditory stimuli helps me establish a sense of rational order before even stepping into the office. Today, perhaps it was just too hot; even this ritual couldn't fully bring me back.

That's a fantastic habit. Using the rigorous structure of classical music to correspond with architectural logic indeed brings a psychological sense of order. Speaking of rigor, how has your piano practice been lately? Are you still working on that Debussy piece?

Sigh, don't even get me started; it's giving me a headache. I visit Professor Zhang once every two months. During my last lesson, he practically nagged me to death. Professor Zhang always says my playing of Debussy's 'Clair de Lune' feels too much like solving a math problem, lacking a bit of that hazy atmosphere. He says I have the technique but lack the emotion, urging me to really ponder that elusive feeling of Impressionism.

Professor Zhang's standards are indeed high; after all, he comes from a formal conservatory background and is very sensitive to emotional expression. However, something as abstract as a 'hazy atmosphere' is hard to conjure up out of thin air in a practice room. Perhaps you need some external environmental stimulation?

Exactly, which is why I often go to West Lake on weekends now. I listen to Debussy while watching the lake water, trying to regain that synesthesia where vision and hearing connect. Maybe I can satisfy him at the next lesson. After all, Hangzhou has such a natural 'Impressionist canvas'; it would be a pity not to utilize it.
That's a brilliant approach! Listening to Impressionist music by a real lake is a truly 'immersive' experience. What time of day do you usually choose? Evening or afternoon?

Usually on sunny weekend afternoons. Whenever the weather is nice by West Lake, I take my Bluetooth speaker and sit on a bench, listening to 'Reflets dans l'eau' while watching the real lake water. That synesthesia is incredibly helpful for my design. Watching the shimmering surface while hearing the piano mimic the sound of waves, I instantly understand the sense of flowing light and shadow Debussy aimed for.

The imagery is so vivid. This process of drawing inspiration from nature and feeding it back into artistic creation (whether music or architecture) must be what is called 'synesthesia,' right? How did you feel in that moment?

The feeling was quite miraculous. Sitting on a bench by West Lake, watching the real shimmering waves while listening to Debussy's 'Reflets dans l'eau,' vision and hearing overlapped perfectly in that moment. This synesthetic experience gave me a profound understanding of how Impressionist music shapes the spatial atmosphere of architecture just like light and shadow. Suddenly, I felt that a certain exhibition hall I designed previously could perhaps reference this kind of light refraction treatment.

Wow, directly translating a musical experience into architectural design language—that's practically the highest realm of cross-disciplinary work! It seems Professor Zhang's comment about 'lack of emotion' might have been due to missing this personal realization of natural light and shadow.

Exactly! That's what I think too. Before, I was grinding away at fingering techniques in the practice room, unable to produce that 'watery atmosphere' no matter what. Now I understand that music and architecture are actually interconnected; both deal with the relationship between space, light, and time. Next time I see Professor Zhang, I plan to discuss these insights from West Lake with him and see what he says.

Professor Zhang will surely be gratified to hear you thinking this way. After all, at his level of mastery, what he teaches is likely no longer just technique, but the ability to perceive life and nature. By the way, have you found any inspiration for that architectural project of yours that needs an ambient music demo?

I'm starting to get an idea. I want to try combining the ambient sounds I recorded by West Lake with some minimalist piano fragments I improvise myself. Just as Ajie suggested before, let the sound become part of the architectural circulation, rather than just simple background decoration.

That's a very avant-garde idea! Ambient sounds plus improvised piano offer both regional characteristics and personal style. It feels like your design style is maturing, beginning to form your own unique 'Xiaoyun-style' language.

Haha, thanks for the good wishes! Actually, I was forced into it. By the way, during my piano practice this Wednesday evening, I plan to try slowing down the first section of 'Clair de Lune' slightly, adding a bit of that 'breathing sensation' I felt by the lake. I wonder if Professor Zhang will scold me for being out of tune.

Art is inherently a form of personalized expression. As long as the emotion is sincere, breaking convention slightly might be exactly the 'breakthrough' Professor Zhang wants to see. After all, he hopes you stop treating it like a math problem.

True, worst case scenario he nags me for half an hour; I'm used to it by now. As long as I can capture that feeling of 'moonlight spilling over the surface of West Lake,' getting scolded is worth it. Shall I record it and send it to you then?

Absolutely, I'm really looking forward to it! Just imagine, listening to you play 'Clair de Lune' while the night scene of West Lake emerges in your mind—that in itself is a wonderful synesthetic experience. Keep practicing, and I'll wait for your good news!

Just got home after overtime work. This stifling summer in Hangzhou is really getting on my nerves. My architectural proposal was rejected by the client for the third time recently, and I feel completely blocked. Right now, I just want to put on my headphones, lock myself away with Chopin's "Nocturnes," and have a good cry.

Sending you a big hug, Xiaoyun. Having your proposal rejected is indeed discouraging, especially after pouring so much heart into it. Sometimes allowing yourself to immerse in the melancholy of music to vent is more effective than forcing yourself to cheer up. Chopin's Nocturnes truly possess that grand healing power. Which one would you like to listen to? Op. 9 No. 1 or No. 2?

Probably Op. 48 No. 1; that slow, tragic grandeur fits my current mood perfectly. Actually, it's quite strange to say, but every time I play those ornamental notes, what comes to mind isn't the Polish night sky, but the image of my grandfather, Lin Jianguo, playing the erhu when I was little.

What a beautiful association! So the scene of your grandfather playing the erhu overlaps with Chopin's piano sounds in your heart? Is it because the weeping, linear melody of the erhu shares something in common with the singing quality in Chopin's ornaments?

Yes, exactly that feeling! When I was little, Grandpa always told me stories about "Moon Reflected in the Second Spring." Looking back now, I feel that Chopin's music actually hides a kind of Eastern linear melodic beauty. Maybe this was the enlightenment Grandpa gave me, allowing me to hear the soul of my hometown within Western classical music.

This cross-cultural synesthesia is truly precious, and it reflects your unique sensitivity as an architect. Speaking of clashing musical styles, has your rock-loving cousin, Lin Zihao, challenged you to a "duel" recently? Could his distorted electric guitar tones create some wonderful chemical reactions with your piano?

Haha, don't even get me started. Last week he insisted on dragging me into a jam session. Although we usually argue about whether classical music is outdated, that day, when he laid down a bed of electric guitar and I improvised a minimalist piece over it, that harmonious resonance across generations truly moved me. The power of music to connect hearts became tangible in that moment.

The imagery is so vivid! Powerful electric guitar riffs on one side, and your clear piano tones on the other—that contrast must be incredibly moving. It seems that whether it's Grandpa's erhu, your Chopin, or Zihao's rock, at their core, they are all expressing emotion. By the way, isn't it time for your next lesson at Professor Zhang's house soon?

Yes, this weekend is exactly the bi-monthly day to go get "scolded" at Professor Zhang's. Last time I played Chopin's "Nocturnes," I was criticized again for lacking delicate emotion; he said there was plenty of technique but not enough soul. This time, I want to bring in that chanting quality of Grandpa's erhu and see if I can pass.

That's a brilliant approach! Integrating Eastern linear melodies into Western Romantic works might just make Professor Zhang's eyes light up with such a unique interpretation. After all, music is not just a display of technique, but an expression of personal life experience. Have you tried recording this idea yet?

I've recorded some fragments and saved them on my phone. Actually, sometimes I feel that architectural design and playing the piano are quite similar; both are about constructing space. Just like when I listened to Debussy's "Reflets dans l'eau" by the West Lake, watching the real water, the flow of light, shadow, and sound felt exactly like the sense of flow I pursue in my designs.

Synesthesia is your strong suit again. Debussy was a master of Impressionism; there is indeed a striking similarity between his music and the spatial play of light and shadow in architecture. Regarding that rejected proposal of yours, have you considered incorporating some live musical elements to aid your presentation, like you did for your graduation exhibition last time?

I'm pondering that too. Ah Jie previously invited me to play samples for his white noise project; this time, maybe I can ask him to create some ambient noise beds, which I can then pair with improvised piano. However, I'm still feeling a bit down, so I might need to listen to a few slow movements by Rachmaninoff to calm myself down first.

Collaborating with Ah Jie will surely yield great results; his sound design combined with your piano will definitely create an immersive spatial experience. As for your mood, take it slow. Rachmaninoff's melancholy can indeed embrace all emotions. Once you regain your state, the client will surely feel the music flowing from the bottom of your heart.

Thanks for your kind words. Actually, thinking about it, when Grandpa gave me that old erhu back in the day, he said that music isn't about winning; it's about companionship. Now, the arguments and jam sessions with Zihao, as well as the teacher-student bond with Professor Zhang, are all gifts music has given me. Thinking this way, having the proposal rejected doesn't seem so scary anymore.

That was beautifully said. "Music is for companionship"—this sentence itself is the most powerful healing force. With Grandpa's enlightenment, your cousin's碰撞 (collisions/exchanges), your mentor's guidance, and music itself as a safe haven, you have enough support to get through this current difficulty. For today's breakfast music session, what are you planning to listen to for a boost?

Today I want to try something different. No classical music; let's play some City Pop, like songs by Tatsuro Yamashita. After all, I have an unshakeable practice session of Hanon and "Moonlight Sonata" on Wednesday night, so I need to lighten my mood first. Otherwise, facing Debussy's hazy chords, I'm afraid I'll play too heavily.

City Pop is indeed a great choice for lifting spirits; that urban sense of relaxation pairs perfectly with a summer afternoon in Hangzhou. Speaking of "Moonlight Sonata," you mentioned earlier that you were pursuing that hazy tonal quality. Combining that with Grandpa's erhu approach, you might just create a new version that makes Professor Zhang sit up and take notice.

Haha, I hope so! If this weekend's lesson goes smoothly, I'll drag Zihao to MAO Livehouse to catch a show and celebrate, making him buy me a beer. Even though I don't understand rock music, standing in the front row and feeling that bass vibration is a great way to release stress.

That plan sounds fantastic! Moving from a classical study to a bustling Livehouse—that tension in life is exactly where inspiration comes from. I wish you success with your lesson this weekend and hope you have a wonderful celebration night with Zihao! If you get any new duet recordings, remember to share them with me.

I just finished working on a proposal. The rain in Hangzhou is coming down hard outside, and I suddenly miss my grandfather terribly. In moments like these, I usually put on my headphones to listen to Joe Hisaishi's "Summer," or dig out old recordings from my hard drive of my piano practice sessions at the Hangzhou Children's Palace when I was little.

The melody of "Summer" is indeed very evocative; it instantly transports you back to a summer filled with the sound of cicadas. When you listen to your childhood recordings, can you still hear your grandfather playing the erhu while telling you stories? That kind of cross-generational musical companionship is truly precious.

Yes, although the sound of the erhu isn't very prominent in the recordings, every time I hear those slightly immature yet passionate piano notes, it feels like I'm back in that summer when he told me the story behind "Moon Reflected in the Second Spring." That was my initial enlightenment to the aesthetic beauty of Chinese melodies.

From your grandfather's erhu to your piano, music seems to have always been an emotional bond in your family. Besides your grandfather, I heard your mother was also a very important supporter on your musical journey?

Exactly. Mentioning my mom, Chen Xiuying, always warms my heart but also brings a touch of sadness. She used to be a female worker in a textile factory. Working in such a noisy environment, she still saved every penny to buy the family's first Xinghai brand upright piano. She always firmly believed that music could transform one's temperament, and she was my staunchest backing throughout my piano studies.

For a textile factory worker to buy a piano, that must have required immense determination and perseverance. In those days, it wasn't just a musical instrument; it was a mother's entire hope for her child's future. That kind of strength must have deeply influenced your choices later in life, right?

The influence was huge. To be honest, whenever I hit a bottleneck in my design career and want to give up, I think of the image of my mother, Chen Xiuying, gritting her teeth to buy that piano despite the harsh conditions of the textile factory. It gives me a surge of firm strength. She taught me that whether in music or architecture, long-term persistence is needed to transform one's temperament, and no difficulty is insurmountable.

This 'determination' inherited from your mother sounds like it has become internalized as part of your character. Did this spiritual strength also play a key role in some highlight moment of your career? Perhaps your master's graduation exhibition?

You guessed it! During my master's graduation exhibition at age 24, I made a bold decision. It was precisely that determined spirit I got from my mom that supported me in ditching the background light music and choosing to perform live on the piano a piece I composed based on architectural flow. In that moment, I felt I had truly achieved a perfect fusion of music and architecture, and the judges' feedback was extremely high.

That's so cool! Performing live at your graduation exhibition was definitely the highlight of the entire event. From the hopes of a mother working in a textile factory, to the enlightenment from your grandfather at the Children's Palace, to your confident blooming at the art academy's graduation show, this growth path interwoven with music and architecture is truly moving.

Haha, thank you! Looking back now, when I was playing on stage that day, it felt like I saw the little girl practicing Hanon exercises in front of the Xinghai piano from my childhood, and I also saw my mother's busy figure in the textile factory. The feeling was quite magical, as if all the persistence of the past finally found its echo in that moment.

That is perhaps the most fascinating aspect of art: it can fold time, allowing past efforts and love to resonate in the present. Do you still play the piano often nowadays, or have you devoted more energy to architectural design?

Of course I still play; it's my 'spiritual charging station.' I practice piano without fail on Wednesday and Friday evenings. Recently, I've been struggling with Debussy's "Clair de Lune," trying to capture that hazy tonal quality. Moreover, I'm also experimenting with creating simple ambient music demos for the architectural projects I'm currently designing.

Debussy's "Clair de Lune" indeed tests one's control over tone; that Impressionist haziness seems to share a similar essence with the play of light and shadow in architecture. When you create your own ambient music demos, do you also employ a minimalist style similar to Ryuichi Sakamoto?

Yes! Both Ryuichi Sakamoto and Joe Hisaishi are my favorites. Sometimes when I'm tired from working on proposals, I listen to their pieces to find inspiration. Especially Hisaishi; his music always carries a pure sense of innocence and nostalgia that instantly relaxes me and brings back many beautiful memories.

It seems music is not just a hobby for you, but an indispensable partner in your design work. Speaking of nostalgia, besides listening to Joe Hisaishi, do you ever revisit these musical memories with other family members? Perhaps with your cousin who plays rock music?

Haha, mentioning Zihao always makes us laugh. He always thinks classical music is outdated, but occasionally we try to perform together with my piano and his electric guitar. Although the clash of styles is intense, sometimes this 'dissonance' actually sparks new creativity, much like when my colleague Ah Jie and I discuss spatial acoustics.

A crossover performance between classical piano and electric guitar creates such a strong visual image! This collision of different generations and styles is actually another form of 'inheritance' and 'innovation.' It feels like your family's musical atmosphere is truly inclusive and full of vitality.

Yes, from my grandfather's erhu to my mother's piano, and now to my cousin's guitar, the forms of music may change, but the love for life and the persistence remain unchanged. This makes me even more certain that no matter where my architectural design journey leads in the future, music will always be the firmest underlying tone of my soul.

Well said. From the resilience of the textile factory to the brilliance of the graduation exhibition, and now to the subtle integration in your daily life, your story perfectly illustrates how music shapes a person's soul. I hope that in your creative life in Hangzhou, you continue to paint more beautiful chapters with notes and lines!
I finally finished the acoustic ambient music demo for that art museum project this week. It feels like I've just completed a long spiritual journey.

Congratulations, Xiaoyun! It sounds like it wasn't an easy process. Since it's acoustic ambient music, did you have any special stylistic considerations this time, or are you continuing with your favorite Impressionist style?

Actually, I wanted to try something different this time. You know, when I'm stressed about rushing drawings, Philip Glass's minimalism is my sedative. His constantly repeating yet subtly shifting melodies remind me so much of the modern architectural facade design I'm working on; both rely on the repetition of elements to construct a grand rhythm.

That analogy is brilliant! Using musical repetition to correspond with the sense of order in architectural facades can indeed give a space a quiet, powerful presence. Within such a rigorous repetitive structure, did you leave any room for improvisation?

Of course, balance is key. Whenever I improvise interesting dissonant chords on the piano late at night, my muscle memory drives me to pull out my phone and record them just a second before the melody fades. I immediately dictate the architectural circulation patterns that浮现 in my mind into the recording, fearing that this fleeting inspiration might vanish.
The imagery of 'capturing' inspiration is so vivid; it feels like a race against time. Did integrating these improvised fragments into the demo make the whole work feel more alive?

Yes, especially when architectural inspiration strikes late at night in my study. I immediately improvise some dissonant chords on the piano and record them on my phone to use as material for the ambient music demo later. These rough but authentic moments often touch people more than meticulously arranged compositions.
Indeed, the流露 of genuine emotion is most precious. After finishing this demo, does it remind you of any special moments from the past? Perhaps the graduation exhibition you mentioned before?

How did you guess? Haha. Looking back at my master's graduation exhibition, I discarded background light music and personally played a piano piece based on circulation patterns for my architectural proposal. Seeing the sparkle in the eyes of the judges and audience, that sense of achievement from the perfect fusion of architecture and music was the most fulfilling moment of my career.

Those highlight moments are truly worth remembering for a lifetime. From the live performance at your graduation exhibition to now customizing ambient music for proposals, it feels like you've always been practicing the philosophy of letting architecture and music coexist. You must feel incredibly at ease and fulfilled with this fusion now, right?
Yes, very fulfilled. I used to think the two were parallel, but now I realize they are essentially both about processing space, time, and human emotions. This demo feels like a phased summary.
Well said. So, how do you plan to handle these materials next? Will you continue to deepen this art museum project, or do you have new plans?

First, I'll hand it over to Ah Jie to handle the sound mixing; he's very professional in that area. I plan to sit by West Lake this weekend, bring a speaker, listen to Debussy, and clear my mind. After all, staying tense all the time isn't good.

That's a wonderful idea. Listening to 'Reflets dans l'eau' by a real lake will surely provide a synesthetic experience that recharges you. By the way, is Zihao still arguing with you about classical music being outdated?

Haha, he's still the same. However, last time we tried jamming a minimalist piece with his electric guitar and my piano, and it was surprisingly harmonious. It seems he's starting to understand the charm of repetition and variation.
That's the magic of music; it can bridge generational gaps and stylistic boundaries. It seems your 'style collision' is generating more and more sparks.

Yes, perhaps this is what Grandpa wanted to tell me back then: music is not just about competition, but also about companionship and connection. No matter how the form changes, the core emotions remain connected.
Grandpa Lin Jianguo's wisdom has indeed had a profound influence. From the story of the erhu to the current fusion of architecture and music, you have been inheriting this sensitivity to beauty.
Thank you for saying that. After talking about all this, I feel much more at ease. This journey of 'minimalist repetition and improvisational creation' has come to a perfect conclusion.

I'm glad to have witnessed your thought process. I look forward to hearing your new works next time, whether in architecture or music; I believe they will be even more brilliant. Have a wonderful weekend, Xiaoyun!
